concise:
Ayah 1–25 · linked video: 01. Al-Inshiqaq (Ayah 1-25)

Surah Al-Inshiqaq: Commentary on Ayahs 1-25

Ayah 1

إِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ ٱنشَقَّتْ

When the sky splits open. This is the 84th surah of the Quran and the final one about which the Prophet ﷺ said that whoever wants to visualize the Day of Resurrection should recite these surahs. Two central themes run through this surah: inevitability and the process by which all of Allah’s ﷻ creation reaches its destined conclusion.

This surah builds directly on the themes established in the previous surahs, particularly Surah At-Takwir and Surah Al-Infitar. In those earlier passages, Allah ﷻ described cosmic upheavals – the sun being wrapped up, the sky tearing open – followed by clear consequences: people would know what they brought forward of deeds. By the time we reach Surah Al-Inshiqaq, the “then what happens” portion is left unstated. You are expected to understand it yourself. The warning has been delivered repeatedly; now you should grasp its meaning on your own.

The word انشقت carries profound significance. In Arabic, this term is specifically used for hard physical objects that crack open – rocks splitting apart, mountains fracturing, the earth breaking open. Unlike words that describe tearing soft materials like cloth (which can be stitched back together), انشقت implies something irreversible. When a rock cracks open, there is no putting it back together. This word choice signals finality – this is the end game.

Throughout the Quran, various terms describe what happens to the sky: فُطِرَت ,انفَطَرَت, فُتِحَت. But انشقت is uniquely irreversible, perfectly suited to describe this final moment when everything comes to its ultimate conclusion.

Ayah 2

وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ

The verb أذنت carries dual meanings in Arabic: to listen carefully and to receive permission. This ayah suggests the sky has been desperately waiting, paying close attention, eager to hear Allah’s ﷻ permission to split open. Imagine a student who has been asking repeatedly for an assignment, waiting eagerly for the teacher’s permission to begin. When the instruction finally comes, they listen intently and act immediately because they’ve been waiting for this moment.

This reveals something profound about the nature of creation: the sky actually wants to tear open. It has been held back only because Allah ﷻ has not yet given permission. Nature as we know it is like a restrained beast, desperate to break free but unable to do so until its Master gives the word. The skies don’t want to stay intact – they are being held together by Allah’s ﷻ command alone.

Why this desperation? The heavens are witnesses to everything that happens on earth. When they observe humanity’s disobedience, their sins and arrogance, they react. In another ayah, when someone claims Allah ﷻ has taken a son, the Quran describes how the heavens are about to tear open from such a blasphemous statement. All of Allah’s ﷻ obedient creation declares His ﷻ perfection and cannot tolerate disobedience. The sky watches our evil deeds and sins, and it wants to react – but it is held back, waiting for permission.

The phrase وحُقَّت means “as it should be” or “rightfully so.” The sky was designed with this ultimate purpose in mind. We typically think of products as being better when they last longer, but some things are designed by their very nature to come to an end. The sky’s destruction is actually when it fulfills its complete purpose. It was always meant to do this. The sky will have fulfilled its right, its obligation, when it finally tears open on that Day.

The passive form انشقت is also significant. It doesn’t mean something external forced the sky to split; rather, it split on its own – doing what it was designed to do all along. The external force – Allah ﷻ – was actually what held it together. Without that divine restraint, it would have fallen apart long ago.

Ayah 3

وَإِذَا ٱلْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ

When the earth is stretched out. The word مُدَّت describes a process of stretching and flattening. Picture a crumpled sheet or piece of leather with highs and lows, valleys and peaks. When someone pulls and stretches it taut, all those variations become flat. This is what happens to the earth – all the mountains above, all the graves below, everything is stretched into a flat plain.

The idea of stretching implies power and control. Someone must have the ability to manipulate the material to stretch it. This is in the passive form, meaning the earth isn’t doing this on its own – Allah ﷻ is stretching it, pulling the earth open and transforming it into a level expanse.

Ayah 4

وَأَلْقَتْ مَا فِيهَا وَتَخَلَّتْ

Here the ayah shifts to active voice – the earth itself throws out whatever has been inside it. The verb ألقت means to throw out forcefully. For ages, the earth has held treasures that people spent their lives seeking. It has held the bodies of the righteous and the sinful alike, buried beneath its surface. Now it can no longer bear their burden. Everything must come out. The secrets of the earth, entire nations buried thousands of years ago that we walk over today without knowing they exist – all of it emerges.

The word تخلّت means to be completely emptied, relieved of a burden, evacuated of contents. The root خلا relates to emptiness, like the empty cells in a honeycomb. When تخلّت is used for a woman, it describes giving birth and finally being relieved of carrying the child. The earth has been pregnant with all these people, holding them inside, and now it cannot take it anymore.

Notice the progression: the earth is stretched (passive – Allah ﷻ does this), and then it throws out its contents (active – the earth does this itself). Allah ﷻ stretches the earth, and as He does, just as a mother’s body stretches to allow the child to emerge, the earth throws out everyone it has been carrying. It is finally relieved.

Ayah 5

وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ

The earth, like the sky, has been seeking permission from its Master. It too has been a witness to the sins of humanity. Every step they took, every word they uttered, every act of disobedience happened on the surface of this earth. The earth itself witnessed Allah’s ﷻ disobedience and could not tolerate it, but Allah ﷻ commanded it to remain calm.

The Quran tells us: “Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what people’s hands have earned” (30:41). The earth reacts to human sin. Sometimes Allah ﷻ allows it to release a little of that reaction, but if the earth reacted to all of humanity’s sins at once, the Day of Judgment would begin immediately. On that Day, He will let it fully respond, which is why it will stretch and refuse to hold anyone inside again.

Now we come to a critical point in the surah. When all of this happens – the sky splitting, the earth stretching and emptying itself – there is no “then” statement. In the previous surahs, such descriptions were followed by clear consequences. Here, you should understand what comes next on your own. The warnings have been given repeatedly; by now, you should know.

Ayah 6

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَـٰقِيهِ

O humanity, you are marching, struggling, toiling toward your Master, and you will certainly meet Him. The word كادح means to struggle upward, to toil with difficulty, like climbing uphill where each step becomes harder. كَدحًا – this masdar form emphasizes continuous, unrelenting effort. You are marching toward Allah ﷻ every single moment, without respite, whether you acknowledge it or not.

Whether you are inside the masjid trying to remember Allah ﷻ or outside engaged in sin, it makes no difference – you are all headed toward Allah ﷻ, and not one of you is slowing down or speeding up. Everyone is moving at the pace Allah ﷻ has decreed. We are all on a conveyor belt moving toward Him, whether we realize it or not, whether we accept it or not, whether we like it or not.

فملاقيه– the word ملاقاة is different from لقاء. While liqa’ means simply to meet, mulaqah means to meet face to face, to stand directly before someone. But here’s the remarkable aspect: the grammatical structure treats this future meeting as though it has already occurred. When you say “he will hit the boy,” that’s future tense. But when you use the construction found here, it’s as if the boy has already been hit – it’s as good as done.

This is why the ayah says فملاقيه – you are as good as having already met Him. Consider the meeting done. Even though you are making progress slowly through the stages of life, imagine the journey is complete and you are already there standing before Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 7

فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ

This ayah takes us directly to the end of that journey, skipping all the intermediate stages. Other places in the Quran describe the stages of human development – from a drop of fluid to development in the womb, from childhood to old age, from life to death to the grave to resurrection. Here, because this surah is about finality and conclusion, Allah ﷻ takes us immediately to the final destination.

As for the one whose book is given to him in his right hand. Notice the passive construction: أُوتِىَ – “has been given.” The book is no longer in your control. Right now, your book is up to you. Then, it will be handed to you, and whatever is written in it will already be written. We saw in previous surahs how this book is constantly being documented. Now it is being handed over.

The word يمين carries multiple layers of meaning. Literally, it means the right hand, and the people of Paradise are called أصحاب اليمين – the people of the right hand, as opposed to أصحاب الشمال – the people of the left hand.

But yamin in Arabic also means fulfilling a promise. The right hand is used across cultures, including Arabic culture, to seal promises and oaths. These are people who kept their covenant with Allah ﷻ. It is only fitting that they should be honored by receiving the book in their right hand, because the right hand represents the oath fulfilled, the promise kept.

Yamin also represents power, control and freedom. “What your right hand possesses” (ما ملكت أيمانكم) refers to what is under your control and disposal. On Judgment Day, nobody has control over anything. Nobody is speaking freely. Everyone is worried only about themselves. But this person, when he receives his book in his right hand, will have the freedom and empowerment to call out, “Come! Look at my book!” (هَاؤُمُ اقْرَءُوا كِتَابِيَهْ) as we learn elsewhere in the Quran. Who will have such freedom on that Day when everyone else is paralyzed with fear?

Finally, the right hand represents honor and dignity. These are people Allah ﷻ has dignified and honored.

The Prophet ﷺ taught us: “Whoever loves to meet Allah, Allah loves to meet them. And whoever dislikes meeting Allah, Allah dislikes meeting them” (Sahih Bukhari). Aisha (RA) questioned this, saying, “We all dislike death – does this mean we dislike meeting Allah?” The Prophet ﷺ clarified: “It’s not like that. When a believer receives the good news of Allah’s ﷻ mercy and His pleasure and Paradise, he looks forward to meeting Allah, and Allah loves to meet him. But when the disbeliever hears that punishment and retribution are coming, he hates that idea, and so Allah hates to meet him as well.”

Ayah 8

فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا

He will receive an easy reckoning. Before explaining what this means, imagine the scene: You’ve received your book in your right hand, but that doesn’t mean you’re finished. The book must still be checked. The angels are standing there, and you approach them showing your book. “This is my book – I got it in the right hand. Did you notice it’s the right hand? Please, on page 75 there’s a major good deed. If you could check page 75… and if you look at page 33, there’s this significant charity… forgive the mistake on page 47, but that particular prayer had excellent khushu’…”

You’re trying to highlight the extra credit assignments, hoping they’ll skip over the not-so-good parts, the prayers where you were yawning, the moments when your focus wandered. Having to present your record for evaluation is nerve-wracking in itself.

Let me share a personal experience to illustrate this. After September 11th, 2001, Muslims received “special treatment” at every airport. I once went through TSA at JFK – not a fun experience at the best of times. I went through security, expecting the usual procedure: “Please step aside, sir, we have a special process for you.” But I happened to go through around 3 PM when the shifts change. The officer was exhausted. I walked through the detector, and he just glanced at me and gestured – go ahead. I was shocked. “Are you sure?” I wanted to ask. He gave me permission to proceed without questioning, and I was stunned by the ease of it.

حساب literally means audit – counting everything line by line. Aisha (RA) asked the Prophet ﷺ about this “easy reckoning.” He explained: “There is not a single person on Judgment Day who is thoroughly questioned except that they will be destroyed. Whoever is questioned in detail on Judgment Day is finished.”

She said, “But Allah ﷻ said they would receive an easy reckoning – what about that?” The Prophet ﷺ clarified: “That just means a ceremonial presentation – showing the book and being allowed to proceed. It’s not a detailed interrogation.”

Think of it like this: you’ve worked hard on an essay, carefully crafting every word. Some teachers barely look at it – they just check the thickness of the paper, make sure there are actual words and not just drawings, and give you full marks. The idea here is that you have this complete record of all your deeds, but Allah ﷻ has instructed the angels: when their record comes, just look at it ceremonially and let them proceed. No detailed questions. No interrogation.

Allah ﷻ is like the easy grader – the teacher who sees you tried your best and gives full credit. Once they get through to the other side, they feel on top of the world.

Ayah 9

وَيَنقَلِبُ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا

He returns to his family full of joy. The scene before was nervously presenting the book, worried and anxious. Now, having passed through successfully, he feels incredible happiness. The word مسرورًا is beautifully chosen. مسرور comes from سرور, which is different from فرح – another word for joy. سرور is happiness that comes from deep within, like a secret sealed inside you. It’s the root of سر meaning secret. This is deeply felt happiness that gives genuine comfort and relaxation, like سرير – a place of rest.

This isn’t superficial happiness, like laughing at a comedy for a few seconds. This is the kind of profound happiness that moves you to tears – the happiness of reunion with your family.

This raises questions: what about people like Nuh (AS) whose family won’t be in Paradise? His wife and son won’t be there. How can the ayah say everyone with this easy reckoning returns to their family?

Allah ﷻ teaches us that family – bonds thicker than blood – are the people who believed with you. For some people, their biological family was their torture in this life, their source of pain. Allah ﷻ will replace them with a much better family. They will feel no loss of love, no loneliness. They will have a complete family to return to.

This ayah also addresses a question many women ask: will they even have a family in Paradise? The answer is yes – ينقلب إلى أهله applies to everyone, men and women. All will have family.

But another question arises: the previous surah showed us that even the drinks of heaven vary in quality. Some have drops of the special drink mixed in, while others drink directly from the fountain by Allah’s ﷻ company. This means there are different levels in Paradise. What if some family members are at much higher levels than others? The distance between one level of Paradise and the next is like the distance from earth to the stars. How will families reunite?

Allah ﷻ addresses this beautifully: “Those who believed and whose descendants followed them in faith – We will join with them their descendants” (52:21). You won’t just meet the family you lived with in this world. You’ll meet your great-great-great-grandfather who was the first in your lineage to accept Islam. You’ll meet your grandmother who died before you were born, your uncle you never knew. It’s a reunion beyond any reunion we can imagine.

Then Allah ﷻ adds: “And We will not deprive them of anything of their deeds” (52:21). If the people at the highest level want to visit family at lower levels, they shouldn’t have to downgrade – that would waste their earned rewards. Allah ﷻ promises He will unite them without taking anything away from their higher status. The only possibility remaining is that those at lower levels receive an upgrade to meet their family at higher stations.

This teaches us something profound: if there are people in our family doing exceptional good work – involved in humanitarian efforts, teaching, helping others – instead of ridiculing them (“Why are you doing social work? You could make so much more money!”), we should support them. They might be our upgrade package. They might be the reason we end up at higher levels in Paradise.

So identify such people in your family and encourage them.

مسرورًا – this deeply felt happiness and comfort when reuniting with family. Sometimes even believing family members didn’t get along in this world. Both were believers, but they had conflicts. The family reunion at Eid can sometimes turn into a family explosion – the worst comes out when certain relatives gather. So you might worry: there’s going to be a giant family reunion in Paradise – is that optional? Can I skip it?

Allah ﷻ removes this concern: “And We will remove whatever is in their hearts of resentment” (15:47). There will be no ill feelings remaining at all. You’ll see that uncle and you won’t have convulsions. You’ll see that cousin and you won’t remember what he said. It will all be gone. Even their voice that used to annoy you will sound sweet.

On the other hand, there are mothers who cry every night for the faith of their son or daughter, begging Allah ﷻ to protect them. They worry so much about their children going through trials in college, facing so many temptations. And finally, when that child also makes it to Paradise, they say: “Indeed, we used to fear for them” – they used to be really terrified about their families. The fact that they say “we used to worry” means they no longer worry. Their family is with them now.

They continue: “So Allah conferred favor upon us and protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Fire” (52:27). Allah ﷻ did them a huge favor by saving and protecting their families, allowing them this reunion on Judgment Day. That itself is a massive blessing from Allah ﷻ. May Allah ﷻ grant all of us this favor.

Ayah 10

وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ وَرَآءَ ظَهْرِهِۦ

As for the one who is given his book behind his back. To understand this imagery, we need to picture the scene of criminals on Judgment Day. The Quran describes them as الذين أجرموا – those who committed crimes – they are chained. They march toward Hellfire as prisoners, with their right hand chained to their neck and their left hand chained behind their back. They cannot move either arm.

In another place, the Quran says such a person receives the book in the left hand. But here, an additional detail is provided: وراء ظهره – behind his back. This means not only is he receiving it in his left hand, but he’s receiving it where he cannot even see it. He cannot open it to check how bad it is. He’s completely blind to his own record.

People behind him can see it and react – “Oh my God, look at what’s written!” – but he himself doesn’t know how bad it is. He’s barricaded even from seeing himself. The previous surah mentioned: “Indeed, from their Lord, that Day, they will be partitioned” (83:15) – they are blocked from seeing their Lord. Now they are also blocked from seeing their own state.

This is the ultimate manifestation of نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ – “They forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves” (9:67). You cannot know your true state when you’ve forgotten your Master. If you truly know your Master, you know yourself. If you don’t know your Master, you don’t know yourself. People who forgot their Master won’t even get to know where they stand on that Day.

Ayah 11

فَسَوْفَ يَدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا

He will cry out for destruction. The word ثبور in Arabic means a few things. One meaning is prison – a place of confinement you cannot escape from. Another is something inescapable, unavoidable. The word also carries connotations of death – specifically, a prison sentence that leads to death.

As soon as this person sees that prison (Hellfire), he begs Allah ﷻ: “Please, just give me the death penalty right now. Don’t let me go in there.” But he will learn what Allah ﷻ tells us in a later surah: لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ – “He will neither die therein nor live” (87:13). You don’t get to die. You don’t get to live either.

Before he’s even taken into the Fire, he’s already pleading: “Just kill me.” In another place, Allah ﷻ says: “Do not call for one destruction today, but call for much destruction” (25:14). When the actual torture begins, they will beg for death again and again and again. When someone is about to be tortured in Hellfire – may Allah ﷻ protect us all – the call will not be for mercy. The call will be for death. Death seems better than what they’re experiencing. فسوف يدعوا ثبورًا.

Ayah 12

وَيَصْلَىٰ سَعِيرًا

He will burn in a Blaze. Allah ﷻ hasn’t even taken him inside yet – he’s asking for death before that happens. Then he will burn in سعير. Throughout the previous surahs, every time Allah ﷻ described Hell, He used the word جحيم. Now, for the first time in this series, He describes it as سعير.

The word سعير comes from سعر – price or value – and from سعّر – to evaluate the price of something. We evaluate gold, diamonds, the quality of coal by burning a piece to test its combustibility. When Hellfire is called سعير, it means on Judgment Day, Allah ﷻ will put it to the test: How well can it burn?

There’s already fire, but now that fire is being pushed to its maximum capacity – like testing a car at full throttle: What’s the top speed? What’s the acceleration? How far can it go? When Allah ﷻ calls Hellfire سعير, it’s the fire that’s been tested to its maximum potential.

Some scholars extrapolate from this that سعير means it will never burn out, because wood burns, coal burns, paper burns, but nothing burns like the fuel it was designed for. Allah ﷻ says: “Its fuel is people and stones” (2:24) – stones because they worshipped stones. The human being is the ultimate fuel that pushes Hellfire to its complete test, its full capacity. May Allah ﷻ protect us all.

Ayah 13

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فِىٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا

He used to be joyful among his family. The same word مسرورًا used for the believer is now used for the disbeliever, but in a completely different context. He used to be really happy when he went back to his family. He had no worries, no concerns.

The word أهل here can mean biological family, but it also means the people you fit in with, the people you belong with, your company. When people deserve one another or are qualified for something, they are أهل to each other. So this could be his family, but it could also be his gang, his cronies, the people he spent all his time with. He was completely content and comfortable among them.

Ayah 14

إِنَّهُۥ ظَنَّ أَن لَّن يَحُورَ

He was convinced that he would never return. The word حور in Arabic means to return to the original state. تحولات are transformations, but حور specifically means returning to what you originally were.

He didn’t think he would ever come back to being a human again. “Once I’m dead, I’m dead. Who’s going to bring me back to life? When we are decayed bones and you can’t even tell what body part they used to be, we’re going to be created as a new creation? That’s impossible.”

The disbelievers asked sarcastically: “Is there only this one life? We die and we live, and nothing destroys us except time. Once time has killed us, there’s no reversing it. We’re not coming back.” He was absolutely convinced he would never be resurrected. That’s why he behaved the way he behaved.

Notice that this surah doesn’t describe what his behavior actually was, because the previous surahs already established that. Now it simply states: he didn’t think he’d ever come back.

The word حور also carries another meaning: hesitation or doubt. لن يحور can also mean he was convinced he would never even doubt or question whether resurrection might happen. That thought didn’t even occur to him. He didn’t allow that consideration to enter his mind at all.

Ayah 15

بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ رَبَّهُۥ كَانَ بِهِۦ بَصِيرًا

But yes! Indeed, his Lord was ever Seeing of him. The word بصير comes from بصر, which is different from نظر. Both mean “to see,” but بصر means to keep your eye on something constantly, to maintain a permanent watch. It’s like the word used for Maryam’s (AS) sister: “And his sister watched from a distance” – maintaining continuous observation.

Allah ﷻ had always been keeping constant watch over him. There was never a moment when Allah ﷻ did not have him under complete observation. كان به بصيرًا – his Lord was always fully aware, constantly watching.

This is the culmination of the entire discussion. He thought he would never be brought back, yet Allah ﷻ was watching him all along. This brings to a close the second and largest section of the surah.

Notice how this section presented the journey’s end without describing all the stages in between – childhood, aging, death, the grave. Other places in the Quran describe those stages. Here, because this surah is about finality, Allah ﷻ takes us directly to the ultimate destination: the book in the right hand or the book behind the back. May Allah ﷻ make us among those with the book in the right hand.

Ayah 16

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلشَّفَقِ

I swear by the twilight. شفق is the orange-reddish glow of the sky about twenty minutes before Maghrib, after Asr time has passed. That light hitting the horizon tells you night is coming. There is no way to save that day anymore – it’s almost Maghrib time. The sun is turning red and its light is being taken away. Darkness is about to settle in.

Ayah 17

وَٱلَّيْلِ وَمَا وَسَقَ

And by the night and what it gathers. وسق means to wrap something up, to gather things and hold them within a fold. جمع means to gather or collect, but وسق specifically means to envelope, to wrap around like a blanket.

Some commentators say the night envelopes the light – wrapping up the entire world like a blanket. Others say the night envelopes the stars inside itself. The image is of night as a blanket with stars tucked inside it. That’s the visual: والليل وما وسق – the night and everything it gathers within its fold.

Ayah 18

وَٱلْقَمَرِ إِذَا ٱتَّسَقَ

And by the moon when it becomes full. اتسق means to gather together, to become complete. The moon as it moves forward, gathering more and more light – the first day’s moon, then the second, then the third – getting thicker and fuller as it gathers more light around itself until it becomes full.

These three oaths have something profound in common: they all describe processes. The day doesn’t suddenly become night. It slowly, gradually transitions. You don’t notice each second passing, but sometimes you look up and realize: “Oh, it’s already almost Maghrib. I haven’t even prayed Asr yet.” Time passes in a process, and before you know it, you’ve reached the end point.

Then there’s Maghrib, and eventually the blue sky turns completely black. والليل وما وسق – by the night and everything it wraps up. Before you know it, the time is gone. You don’t even realize how quickly it happens.

Think about Ramadan. It starts and feels like it will last forever because we’ve only had one fast. But before you know it – two weeks have already passed. How did that happen so quickly?

والقمر إذا اتسق – things that follow a process creep up on you seemingly out of nowhere. You’re sitting with your children and suddenly you look at them: “When did you get this tall? When did that happen?”

This notion of things moving gradually toward their conclusion – that’s what these oaths establish.

Ayah 19

لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقًا عَن طَبَقٍ

You will surely ride stage after stage. This is the response to the oaths. Just as the day transitions through stages to night, just as the moon moves through phases to fullness, you are absolutely going to progress through one stage after another after another.

The Prophet ﷺ said this ayah was about himself. Early in Makkah, nobody would listen to what he was saying. Things were only getting worse and worse, like the night getting darker and darker. The light of Islam was like the slightest crescent moon. But eventually that moon would gain more and more momentum, and the light would shine brighter and brighter. Eventually even that moon wouldn’t be enough – the entire night would be replaced by full daylight.

“Yes, you’re going through struggles right now. There’s persecution in Makkah right now. There will even be attempts to hurt and kill you. But eventually there will be Hijrah. Then there will be Badr. Then Uhud. Then Ahzab. Then Hudaybiyyah. Then the conquest of Makkah. It will take stages – it doesn’t happen overnight. Each level, you have to cross through one after another.”

The other meaning applies to all of us: whether you like it or not, you are going from one stage to the next to the next, and you have no say in it. You were inside your mother’s womb – you loved it there, but you had to leave. When you were a baby being carried everywhere, eventually you had to walk. Then you had to talk. Then you were thrown into school whether you liked it or not. You grew older whether you liked it or not.

The hair starts changing colors – you can dye it all you want, but you know what’s really happening. Some of that hair starts quitting its job, saying “I’m done, goodbye” – and you can do nothing about it. You can put in fake plugs if you want, but it’s going. The skin starts getting wrinkly. You can do nothing about it. Eyesight weakens. Bones become more brittle. You don’t move like you used to. You can’t sit like you used to. You can’t stand or pray like you used to. You won’t be able to remember things. Your memory starts failing.

One stage after another after another. Your back will bend. You’ll be looking down toward the ground where you’re going to end up anyway. Then you’ll be in that grave, and you can’t do anything about it. Your body will be washed, my body will be washed, and it won’t be in our control. We’ll be inside that patch of dirt. Then Allah ﷻ will pull us out and we’ll have no say. Stage after stage after stage after stage – you are headed toward Allah ﷻ.

This ayah echoes the earlier statement: يا أيها الإنسان إنك كادح إلى ربك كدحًا – the same concept explained again through these oaths.

Ayah 20

فَمَا لَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

Then what is wrong with them that they do not believe? If this progression through stages is so clear to them – they’re getting older, the sun sets every day, the night comes, the moon goes through its phases – why don’t they believe? They see it happening. This isn’t hidden evidence. They witness it constantly. Yet still they don’t believe.

Ayah 21

وَإِذَا قُرِئَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ لَا يَسْجُدُونَ ۩

And when the Quran is recited to them, they do not prostrate. This is a place where there is scholarly difference of opinion about whether we should make sajdah, but we are on the side of caution and perform it.

When the Quran is recited to them, they don’t fall into prostration. They don’t react. Let me remind you of something from the beginning of this surah: the skies were desperately waiting for Allah’s ﷻ word, ready to tear open. The earth was desperately waiting for Allah’s ﷻ word so it could stretch and throw out everything inside it. Everything else in creation is desperate to respond to Allah’s ﷻ word.

Here you have human beings listening to the word of Allah ﷻ, and they don’t even budge. The Quran is recited to them and they don’t fall into sajdah. The contrast is stark. Everything else desperately wants to hear and respond to Allah’s ﷻ word, but these humans remain unmoved.

لا يسجدون doesn’t only mean the physical act of putting your head on the ground. It means submitting yourself to Allah’s ﷻ instructions, even when they go against your greed, your desires, your worldly ambitions.

Ayah 22

بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُكَذِّبُونَ

Rather, those who disbelieve deny. The word يكذبون is intensive – they want to call all of it a lie. Not يكذبون which would mean simply “they lie,” but يكذبون – they are determined to call everything a lie. The Prophet ﷺ is a liar. The Quran is a lie. The promise of resurrection is a lie. All of it – lies.

Why do they want to call it all lies? Because these teachings are getting in their way. People want to do certain things, earn certain things, profit in certain ways. When the teachings of our religion get in the way of those pursuits, the easy solution is: “I don’t want to deal with this. It’s all lies.”

Ayah 23

وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُوعُونَ

And Allah is most knowing of what they hoard. يوعون comes from وعاء – a container or bag. In the verb form أوعى, it means to hoard something, to keep amassing and storing away in containers. This builds on the theme from the previous surah about people who hoard wealth even by cheating their customers a little.

When people become greedy, when they just want to earn and earn and earn, and then criticism comes from Allah ﷻ, they don’t want to submit. لا يسجدون expands in meaning – it’s not just about putting your head on the ground. It’s about submitting to Allah’s ﷻ instructions even when they conflict with your greed.

Let me take a moment to help you understand this point, because it’s crucial. The people of Makkah didn’t just worship idols. They worshipped money. Each idol was a representative of a different tribe. Each tribe would come to Makkah to pay respect to their idol. This created a religious tourism industry. That entire tribe would come, do their business, buy and sell, stay in hotels – the whole hospitality industry.

All of Makkah’s wealth came because the idols were there. No tribe would attack Makkah because their idol was there. The idols provided political security and economic security. When they refused to remove the idols, it was because removing them would destroy their income, their entire economic system.

يوعون – they were bagging, hoarding, profiting. Allah ﷻ knows what they’re accumulating. يوعون can mean spiritually – Allah ﷻ knows better what deeds they’re gathering. They think they’re doing good, but they’re actually doing terrible things: يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا – “thinking they are doing good” (18:104). Allah ﷻ knows better than they do what they’re truly earning.

But there’s also a sharp political and economic commentary here on the corruption of the Quraysh leadership. أوعى also means to hide and keep away secretly – they’re doing all of this but showing a different picture. Allah ﷻ will later expose their false religiosity: “Woe to those who pray, who are heedless of their prayer, those who make show [of their deeds] and withhold [simple] assistance” (107:4-7). They pray, but only for show. The reality is they don’t care about a single orphan or needy person. Yet they’re the custodians of religion. That’s their reality.

Ayah 24

فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

So give them tidings of a painful punishment. بشّر typically means to give good news, glad tidings. Here it’s used sarcastically: “Congratulate them.” Give them the good news that a painful punishment is coming their way.

This is a devastating irony – using the word for good news to announce their doom.

Ayah 25

إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍۭ

Except those who believe and do righteous deeds – for them is a reward uninterrupted. This exception can be understood two ways grammatically:

First, as استثناء متصل – a connected exception: “Congratulate them all with painful punishment, except those among them who decide to repent, convert, believe and do good deeds. They will be rewarded.” This highlights that the door of repentance is always open.

Second, as استثناء منفصل – a disconnected exception: “All of them will be punished except those who have already believed” – meaning the Muslims are being highlighted separately. All will face punishment except the believers.

Then comes my favorite word in this entire surah: غير ممنون. The word منّ in Arabic has two meanings.

First, منّ from قطع – to cut. قطع المنّة means to cut something off, to discontinue it. غير ممنون means the reward Allah ﷻ will give them will never be discontinued. It will keep going and growing. Your iman, your good deeds, your salah, the good you do, the truth you speak – all of it will continue growing in your scales. The reward will never be taken away. Nobody can steal it from you. It’s endless, uninterrupted, never cut off.

Second, منّ also means to remind someone that you did them a favor. Allah ﷻ warns us: لَا تُبْطِلُوا صَدَقَاتِكُم بِالْمَنِّ – “Do not invalidate your charities by reminders [of it]” (2:264). When you give someone something and then keep reminding them – “Enjoying that present I gave you? It was pretty expensive, you know” – you make them feel degraded.

غير ممنون means nobody will ever make you feel that this reward is a grudging favor. You’ll be honored in the way you’re rewarded. It will be dignified. Only Allah ﷻ will remind you that this is His favor, but He does so in a way that honors you, not degrades you.

This brings us to the close of this beautiful, powerful surah – a surah about finality, about the inevitable journey we’re all on toward Allah ﷻ, and about the two ultimate destinations that await us. May Allah ﷻ make us among those who receive their book in the right hand, who have an easy reckoning, who return to their families in joy, and who receive a reward that is uninterrupted and given with honor.
deep:
Ayah 1–25 · linked video: 01. Al-Inshiqaq (Ayah 1-25)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

انشَقَّتْ (inshaqat) [ش-ق-ق]

The root ش-ق-ق means to split, crack or tear open. The verb انشَقَّتْ (inshaqat) is in Form VII (infi’al), which indicates something happening to itself reflexively. This form suggests that the sky will crack open on its own, not by an external force. The choice of شَقَّ over other words for tearing (like فَطَرَ) is significant because شَقَّ is used for hard objects like rocks and mountains - things that once cracked cannot be stitched back together. This represents an irreversible, final destruction. When you say انشَقَّت, it means the sky fell apart on its own doing what it was meant to do all along, as if Allah’s permission was the only thing holding it together.

السَّمَاءُ (as-sama) [س-م-و]

The root س-م-و relates to height and elevation. السَّمَاءُ (as-sama) refers to the sky or heaven - everything above us. The definite article “al” emphasizes this specific sky we know and see daily.

Ayah 2

أَذِنَتْ (adhinat) [أ-ذ-ن]

The root أ-ذ-ن relates to listening and permission. The verb أَذِنَتْ has two interconnected meanings: to listen carefully and to receive permission. The form used here suggests the sky has been eagerly waiting to hear words of permission from Allah to tear open. It’s like a student desperately asking for permission to do something and finally receiving it - they listen with eager anticipation. This word implies the sky has been wanting to destroy itself but has been held back because Allah hasn’t given permission yet.

رَبِّهَا (rabbiha) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means master, lord, sustainer and nurturer. The possessive pronoun “ha” (her/its) refers back to the sky, indicating the sky’s Master.

حُقَّتْ (huqqat) [ح-ق-ق]

The root ح-ق-ق means truth, right and what is proper. حُقَّتْ is in the passive form meaning “it was made right” or “as it should be.” The phrase وَحُقَّتْ indicates this is the sky’s rightful purpose - it was actually designed to tear open on that day. When it destroys itself, it’s fulfilling its ultimate purpose, as if this beautiful sky was purposeless until the moment it tears open.

Ayah 3

مُدَّتْ (muddat) [م-د-د]

The root م-د-د means to stretch, extend or pull. The passive form مُدَّتْ indicates the earth is being stretched by an external force (Allah). This is unlike the sky which tears on its own - here Allah is actively pulling and stretching the earth. The image is of someone pulling leather or a sheet until all the wrinkles and unevenness become flat. All the highs (mountains) and lows (graves) will be flattened into a plain field.

الأَرْضُ (al-ard) [أ-ر-ض]

The root أ-ر-ض refers to the earth, land or ground - the surface we walk upon.

Ayah 4

أَلْقَتْ (alqat) [ل-ق-ي]

The root ل-ق-ي means to throw, cast out or expel. أَلْقَتْ indicates a forceful throwing out. The earth will throw out everything that has been inside it - treasures people have been searching for, the dead buried for thousands of years, entire civilizations buried underground.

تَخَلَّتْ (takhallat) [خ-ل-و]

The root خ-ل-و means to be empty, vacant or relieved of something. تَخَلَّتْ (Form V) means to empty oneself completely, to be relieved or freed from something. This word is also used for when a woman gives birth and is finally relieved. The earth has been pregnant with all these people and things inside it, and it can no longer tolerate carrying them. When it stretches and throws everything out, it’s like giving birth - finally being relieved of a burden it could no longer bear.

Ayah 6

كَادِحٌ (kadihun) [ك-د-ح]

The root ك-د-ح means to struggle, toil, labor or march with difficulty. كَادِحٌ is an active participle indicating continuous action. The human being is constantly struggling and marching, like going uphill where it gets harder and harder. This word suggests tumbling forward, never getting a break, constantly making progress whether you want to or not.

رَبِّكَ (rabbika) [ر-ب-ب]

Your Master - the possessive form makes it personal, emphasizing that each person has an individual relationship with their Lord.

كَدْحًا (kadhan) [ك-د-ح]

The verbal noun (masdar) from the same root, emphasizing the continuous nature of this struggle. The repetition of the root (verb and masdar together) is called maf’ul mutlaq, which intensifies the meaning - you are absolutely, continuously struggling.

مُلَاقِيهِ (mulaqihi) [ل-ق-ي]

The root ل-ق-ي means to meet face to face. مُلَاقِيهِ is an active participle with the possessive pronoun, meaning “one who will meet Him.” The word لِقَاء suggests meeting someone directly, face to face, not at a distance. The grammatical construction here (with the idafah to the pronoun “hi”) makes it as if the meeting has already happened - consider it done, it’s a promise already fulfilled.

Ayah 7

أُوتِيَ (utiya) [أ-ت-ي]

The root أ-ت-ي means to come or bring. The passive form أُوتِيَ means “was given” - indicating the person has no control, the book is being handed to them. Right now the book is in our control (we’re writing it), but then it will no longer be in our control.

كِتَابُهُ (kitabuhu) [ك-ت-ب]

The root ك-ت-ب means to write. كِتَابُهُ means “his book” - the record of deeds that has been written and constantly documented.

بِيَمِينِهِ (bi-yaminihi) [ي-م-ن]

The root ي-م-ن means right (side), and يَمِين specifically means the right hand. But يَمِين has multiple layers of meaning: (1) literally the right hand, (2) fulfilling a promise or oath (the right hand represents keeping your word), (3) power and control (the right hand represents authority and freedom), and (4) honor and dignity. These people kept their oath with Allah, have power and freedom on Judgment Day to speak, and have been honored and dignified by Allah.

Ayah 8

يُحَاسَبُ (yuhasabu) [ح-س-ب]

The root ح-س-ب means to calculate, count or reckon. The passive form يُحَاسَبُ means “he will be audited/questioned.” حِسَاب means an accounting or audit where things are counted line by line.

حِسَابًا (hisaban) [ح-س-ب]

The verbal noun from the same root, emphasizing the action of being audited.

يَسِيرًا (yasiran) [ي-س-ر]

The root ي-س-ر means easy, simple or facilitated. يَسِيرًا means easy, gentle, or light. According to the hadith from the Prophet ﷺ, this “easy audit” means the angels will ceremonially look at the book and let the person go - not a detailed interrogation. Anyone who is truly questioned in detail about their deeds will be destroyed.

Ayah 9

يَنقَلِبُ (yanqalibu) [ق-ل-ب]

The root ق-ل-ب means to turn, return or flip over. Form VII (inqalaba) means to return or go back. The verb suggests returning to where you belong or came from.

أَهْلِهِ (ahlihi) [أ-ه-ل]

The root أ-ه-ل means family, people or those you belong with. أَهْل refers to family but also to people you fit in with, people you deserve, those who are your companions. This could be biological family or the family of believers - those who truly have a bond thicker than blood.

مَسْرُورًا (masruran) [س-ر-ر]

The root س-ر-ر means joy, happiness or secret. مَسْرُورًا means filled with happiness, but specifically a deep, internal happiness (سُرُور) - not superficial joy but the kind that moves you to tears, the happiness felt at a reunion with loved ones. It comes from the same root as سَرِير (bed/comfort) and سِرّ (secret) - suggesting comfort, relaxation and deeply felt emotion.

Ayah 10

أُوتِيَ (utiya) [أ-ت-ي]

Same as Ayah 7 - the passive form meaning “was given.”

كِتَابُهُ (kitabuhu) [ك-ت-ب]

Same as Ayah 7 - “his book.”

وَرَاءَ (wara’a) [و-ر-ي]

The root و-ر-ي relates to being behind or concealed. وَرَاءَ means behind or at the back. Combined with ظَهْرِهِ (his back), this creates the image of the book being given behind the person’s back where they cannot see it.

ظَهْرِهِ (dhahrihi) [ظ-ه-ر]

The root ظ-ه-ر means back, surface or what is apparent. ظَهْر specifically means the back of the body. The criminals are chained with their right hand to their neck and left hand behind their back, so receiving the book behind the back means they cannot even see how bad their record is - they’re blinded from even knowing themselves.

Ayah 11

يَدْعُو (yad’u) [د-ع-و]

The root د-ع-و means to call, invoke or cry out. يَدْعُو indicates he will call out or cry for something.

ثُبُورًا (thuburan) [ث-ب-ر]

The root ث-ب-ر means destruction, ruin or death. ثُبُور specifically means total destruction or a death sentence. In Arabic, ثُبُور is also used as a word for prison (المَثْبُرَة) - specifically an inescapable prison or a prison sentence leading to death. When the person sees hellfire, he begs for death immediately, calling “Ya Thuburan!” - “O destruction, come to me now before I enter that fire!”

Ayah 12

يَصْلَىٰ (yasla) [ص-ل-ي]

The root ص-ل-ي means to burn, roast or be exposed to intense heat. يَصْلَىٰ means he will burn in or be exposed to the fire.

سَعِيرًا (sa’iran) [س-ع-ر]

The root س-ع-ر means to kindle, ignite or evaluate the price/quality of something. سَعِير is the blazing flame or intense fire. The word comes from تَسْعِير - evaluating the quality of something by testing it (like testing coal by burning it). When hellfire is called سَعِير, it means it’s being put to its ultimate test, its maximum capacity - and the human being is its ultimate fuel, what makes it burn at its peak intensity.

Ayah 13

مَسْرُورًا (masruran) [س-ر-ر]

The same word used in Ayah 9 for the believer’s happiness, but now describing the disbeliever’s false happiness in this worldly life. He was filled with joy among his family and companions, thinking this life would never end.

أَهْلِهِ (ahlihi) [أ-ه-ل]

Same as Ayah 9 - but here referring to his family or his gang, his cronies, the people he surrounded himself with in wrongdoing.

Ayah 14

يَحُورَ (yahuru) [ح-و-ر]

The root ح-و-ر means to return to the original state or to hesitate/doubt. يَحُورَ means to come back or return. The phrase لَن يَحُورَ means “he will never return” - he was convinced he would never be brought back to life after death, never return to his original state as a living human being. The word also carries the meaning of doubt - he never even allowed the thought to occur to him that resurrection might be possible.

Ayah 15

بَصِيرًا (basiran) [ب-ص-ر]

The root ب-ص-ر means to see or watch. بَصِيرًا is an intensive form meaning “all-seeing” or “keeping constant watch.” The phrase كَانَ بِهِ بَصِيرًا means Allah had always been watching him constantly, keeping him in full view. Unlike أَبْصَرَ (to see temporarily), بَصِيرًا emphasizes continuous, unwavering observation - Allah never stopped watching him for a moment.

Ayah 16

الشَّفَقِ (ash-shafaq) [ش-ف-ق]

The root ش-ف-ق relates to the reddish glow and compassion. الشَّفَق specifically means the red or orange glow of the sky at twilight, about 20 minutes before Maghrib. This is when the day is almost over, the light is being taken away, and darkness is about to settle in. It represents the final stages before the end.

Ayah 17

اللَّيْلِ (al-layl) [ل-ي-ل]

The root ل-ي-ل means night or darkness. اللَّيْل is the night.

وَسَقَ (wasaqa) [و-س-ق]

The root و-س-ق means to gather, collect or wrap up. وَسَقَ means to envelop or take things into its fold. The phrase وَمَا وَسَقَ means “and what it gathers/envelops” - the night wraps up the world like a blanket, enveloping the stars inside it.

Ayah 18

الْقَمَرِ (al-qamar) [ق-م-ر]

The root ق-م-ر refers to the moon. الْقَمَر is the moon.

اتَّسَقَ (ittasaqa) [و-س-ق]

From the same root و-س-ق as in the previous ayah, but Form VIII (iftaala). اتَّسَقَ means to become full, complete or gather together. The phrase إِذَا اتَّسَقَ refers to when the moon becomes full - as it moves through its phases gathering more and more light, getting thicker and fuller.

Ayah 19

لَتَرْكَبُنَّ (latarkabunna) [ر-ك-ب]

The root ر-ك-ب means to ride or mount upon. The form here includes emphatic particles (lam + noon of emphasis) making it “you will absolutely ride/progress.” This means to advance, mount up, or move forward.

طَبَقًا (tabaqan) [ط-ب-ق]

The root ط-ب-ق means layer, level or stage. طَبَقًا عَن طَبَقٍ means “layer upon layer” or “stage after stage” - one level on top of another, progressing through different phases. This refers both to the stages of the Prophet’s ﷺ mission (from persecution in Makkah to victory) and to the inevitable stages every human passes through from birth to death to resurrection.

Ayah 20

لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (la yu’minuna) [أ-م-ن]

The root أ-م-ن means to believe, be secure or have faith. The negative يُؤْمِنُونَ لَا means “they do not believe” - despite all the evidence around them (the stages, the sunset, the night), they still refuse to believe.

Ayah 21

قُرِئَ (quri’a) [ق-ر-أ]

The root ق-ر-أ means to read or recite. The passive form قُرِئَ means “is recited” - when the Quran is recited to them.

الْقُرْآنُ (al-Quran) [ق-ر-أ]

From the same root - the Quran, that which is recited.

يَسْجُدُونَ (yasjuduna) [س-ج-د]

The root س-ج-د means to prostrate or submit. يَسْجُدُونَ means “they prostrate.” The phrase لَا يَسْجُدُونَ means they don’t fall into prostration - they don’t submit, don’t react, don’t humble themselves. While the sky and earth are desperate to hear and respond to Allah’s word, these humans hear it and don’t even budge.

Ayah 22

يُكَذِّبُونَ (yukadhhibuna) [ك-ذ-ب]

The root ك-ذ-ب means to lie or deny the truth. Form II يُكَذِّبُونَ (with emphasis through doubling the middle letter) means they consider everything a lie, they reject it all as false. The form suggests intensity - they don’t just reject one thing, they call all of it lies: the Prophet ﷺ, the Quran, the resurrection, everything.

Ayah 23

يُوعُونَ (yu’una) [و-ع-ي]

The root و-ع-ي means to preserve, gather or hoard in a container. The verb يُوعُونَ means “they gather/hoard.” This refers to hoarding wealth, constantly amassing more and more. It builds on the theme from the previous surah about those who hoard and cheat. When people become greedy and just want to earn more and more, they don’t want Allah’s instructions to get in their way, so they reject submission to Allah.

Ayah 24

بَشِّرْهُم (bashshirhum) [ب-ش-ر]

The root ب-ش-ر means to give good news or glad tidings. The command بَشِّرْهُم normally means “give them good news,” but here it’s used ironically - “congratulate them” or “give them the good news” of painful punishment. It’s sarcastic, highlighting the contrast between what they expect and what they’ll receive.

عَذَابٍ (adhabin) [ع-ذ-ب]

The root ع-ذ-ب means punishment or torment. عَذَابٍ means punishment.

أَلِيمٍ (alimin) [أ-ل-م]

The root أ-ل-م means pain or hurt. أَلِيمٍ means painful, agonizing.

Ayah 25

آمَنُوا (amanu) [أ-م-ن]

The root أ-م-ن means to believe or have faith. آمَنُوا means “they believed.”

عَمِلُوا (amilu) [ع-م-ل]

The root ع-م-ل means to do or work. عَمِلُوا means “they did” or “they worked.”

الصَّالِحَاتِ (as-salihat) [ص-ل-ح]

The root ص-ل-ح means to be righteous, good or proper. الصَّالِحَاتِ means righteous deeds, good works.

أَجْرٌ (ajrun) [أ-ج-ر]

The root أ-ج-ر means reward or compensation. أَجْرٌ means a reward.

مَمْنُونٍ (mamnunin) [م-ن-ن]

The root م-ن-ن has two key meanings: (1) to cut or discontinue (from مَنَنَ - to cut), and (2) to remind someone of a favor done to them. غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ therefore means: (1) never discontinued, never cut off, endless and ever-growing, and (2) never will they be reminded of it as a favor, never made to feel degraded - it’s a dignified gift where only Allah tells them it’s His favor, but they won’t be made to feel inferior by receiving it.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 1

The verb انشَقَّتْ is in Form VII, which grammatically indicates reflexive action - the subject acts upon itself. This is crucial because it shows the sky is not being torn by an external force; rather, it’s tearing itself apart. Had it been شُقَّتْ (passive of Form I), it would mean something external was tearing it. The choice of Form VII emphasizes that the sky has always wanted to do this, and Allah’s permission is the only thing holding it back.

Ayah 2

The phrase وَحُقَّتْ employs a passive verb in a nominal sentence structure for emphasis. The passive voice (it was made right/proper) removes any agent and makes this an absolute statement about the nature of reality itself. The phrase creates a parenthetical comment, an interruption in the narrative that forces the reader to pause and consider: this is exactly as it should be, this is the sky’s true purpose.

Ayah 4

The dual verbs أَلْقَتْ and تَخَلَّتْ create a powerful rhetorical effect. The first (threw out) is sudden and forceful, while the second (emptied itself) suggests relief and completion. This progression mirrors the metaphor of childbirth - the forceful expulsion followed by the relief of finally being emptied. The sound pattern also contributes: the qaf (ق) in alqat is a harsh, forceful sound, while the softer kha (خ) in takhallat suggests release.

Ayah 5

The surah breaks pattern here by NOT providing the “then” clause (jawab al-shart) that listeners expect after four “when” clauses (idha statements). This ellipsis is rhetorically powerful - by the fourth surah in this series, Allah expects the audience to know what happens next. The omission forces active engagement and reflection, making the meaning more impactful than if it were spelled out.

Ayah 6

The phrase كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا uses maf’ul mutlaq (cognate accusative) - pairing the active participle kadihun with its verbal noun kadhan. This repetition intensifies the meaning: you are absolutely, continuously struggling. The structure emphasizes inevitability and constancy - there’s no escape from this forward march.

The shift from third person in the opening ayahs to second person here (rabbika - YOUR Lord) suddenly makes the message direct and personal, pulling the listener into the narrative.

Ayah 7-10

The surah employs perfect parallelism (muqabalah) between the two types of people: those who receive the book in the right hand (Ayahs 7-9) and those who receive it behind their back (Ayahs 10-12). Each group gets three ayahs with matching grammatical structures:

  • Ayah 7 and 10 both begin with أُوتِيَ كِتَابُهُ (was given his book)
  • Ayah 8 and 11 describe what happens next (fa-sawfa + verb)
  • Ayah 9 and 13 describe their state (using similar emotional descriptors)

This symmetry highlights the stark contrast between the two destinies.

Ayah 8

The phrase حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا uses an indefinite noun (hisaban) followed by an adjective (yasiran), creating emphasis through definiteness contrast. The masdar (hisaban) being indefinite suggests “some kind of reckoning” while the adjective yasiran qualifies it as specifically easy. This grammatical structure mirrors the meaning: yes there’s an audit, but it’s an easy one.

Ayah 9

The verb يَنقَلِبُ (Form VII) again indicates self-initiated action - he returns on his own, freely, not being forced. This contrasts with those who will be dragged to their punishment. The choice of yanqalibu over yarju (returns) suggests a complete transformation or flipping over - returning to a state you truly belong in.

Ayah 10

The prepositional phrase وَرَاءَ ظَهْرِهِ (behind his back) is positioned for emphasis before the verb. Arabic word order allows flexibility, and placing this phrase first highlights the shameful, hidden nature of how the book is given. The person cannot even see what’s being done to them.

Ayah 13-14

These ayahs use إِنَّهُ (innahu) at the start of Ayah 13 and ظَنَّ (he thought) at the start of Ayah 14 - both are emphatic particles in Arabic grammar. The إِنَّ emphasizes the reality of his worldly happiness, while ظَنَّ (he assumed/thought) emphasizes his false certainty about never returning. The contrast between reality (his happiness) and delusion (his certainty about death) is grammatically embedded.

Ayah 15

The entire ayah is a nominal sentence (jumla ismiyya) starting with بَلَىٰ (Nay/Indeed), which is a particle that negates a previous negative. This structure emphatically refutes the false assumption in Ayah 14. The phrase كَانَ بِهِ بَصِيرًا uses kana (was) to indicate a continuous state in the past - Allah had ALWAYS been watching. The ba (بِهِ) preposition intensifies the meaning: not just watching generally, but watching HIM specifically.

Ayah 16-18

These three ayahs begin with fala (فَلَا), introducing oaths (qasam). The particle la is emphatic, and using it three times creates a powerful rhythmic structure: - فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِالشَّفَقِ - وَاللَّيْلِ وَمَا وَسَقَ
- وَالْقَمَرِ إِذَا اتَّسَقَ

Each oath is shorter and more forceful than the last, building momentum. The grammatical particle إِذَا (when) with اتَّسَقَ creates a conditional-temporal clause, emphasizing the process of the moon becoming full rather than just its full state.

Ayah 19

The response to the oaths uses the emphatic lam plus doubled nun: لَتَرْكَبُنَّ. This is one of the strongest forms of emphasis in Arabic - “you will ABSOLUTELY ride/progress.” The phrase طَبَقًا عَن طَبَقٍ uses the preposition عَن (from/upon) to show succession: one stage coming from or upon another. This isn’t just stages side by side, but one emerging from the previous in sequence.

Ayah 20-21

Both ayahs use the interrogative مَا (what) + negative particle to create rhetorical questions: فَمَا لَهُمْ (What is with them?) and إِذَا قُرِئَ عَلَيْهِمُ (When it is recited TO them). The use of عَلَيْهِمْ (upon them) rather than لَهُمْ (to them) suggests the Quran is being imposed upon them from above, yet they still don’t respond. The present tense verbs (يُؤْمِنُونَ لَا, يَسْجُدُونَ لَا) indicate continuous, habitual refusal.

Ayah 22

The verb يُكَذِّبُونَ is Form II, which in Arabic creates intensity and repetition. They don’t just deny once; they continuously, emphatically call everything lies. The Form II pattern (fa’’ala) doubles the middle consonant, phonetically emphasizing the intensity of their rejection.

Ayah 24

The imperative بَشِّرْهُم (give them glad tidings) is used ironically. Grammatically it’s a command for good news, but semantically it announces punishment. This rhetorical device (irony/sarcasm) is called تَهَكُّم in Arabic rhetoric - using language that typically conveys one meaning to actually convey its opposite, which intensifies the impact.

Ayah 25

The exception structure إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (except those who believed) uses istithna (exception). This grammatical structure can be understood as either muttasil (connected - meaning “except those of them who believed,” giving them a chance to repent) or munfasil (disconnected - meaning “except those who already believed,” referring to the Muslims as a separate group). Both grammatical readings are valid and produce different theological meanings.

The phrase غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ uses ghayr (non/without) plus the passive participle, creating a double negative that becomes strongly positive: not un-discontinued = absolutely continuous. This grammatical construction (double negative for emphasis) is called litotes in English but is a natural feature of Arabic rhetoric that intensifies the positive meaning.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 1-5

These ayahs about the sky tearing and earth stretching connect to the eschatological descriptions found throughout the Quran and build upon previous surahs in this section of the Quran. Surah At-Takwir (81) described the sun being wrapped up, stars falling, mountains being set in motion - all cosmic upheavals marking the Day of Resurrection. Surah Al-Infitar (82) described the sky being torn (fatarat) and split open. Now Al-Inshiqaq uses even stronger language with “inshaqat” (cracked irreversibly), representing the final, ultimate description in this series.

The concept of creation witnessing human deeds and reacting to them has Quranic precedent. When people claim Allah has taken a son, the Quran states: “The heavens are about to be torn apart because of it” (19:90). The heavens and earth are described as conscious witnesses who will testify on Judgment Day.

The stretching of the earth (muddat) parallels Biblical eschatology, though the Quran’s description is unique. In Biblical tradition, there are references to the earth being shaken and mountains being leveled on the Day of Judgment (Isaiah 40:4, Revelation 16:20), but the Quranic image of the earth being stretched like leather or cloth to become a plain field is distinctive.

Ayah 7-15

The imagery of receiving the book in the right hand versus behind the back reflects the social customs of Arabia where receiving something in the right hand indicated honor and acceptance, while the left hand or behind the back indicated disgrace. The hadith literature extensively develops this Day of Judgment imagery, with detailed descriptions of how people will receive their records.

The phrase “his master had always been watching him” (inna rabbahu kana bihi basiran) echoes themes throughout the Quran about Allah’s constant observation of humanity. This is the culmination of the witness theme: the sky witnesses, the earth witnesses, angels record deeds, and above all, Allah Himself observes everything.

Ayah 16-18

The oaths by the twilight, night and full moon were particularly significant to the original Arabian audience. These natural phenomena marked time for a people without modern timekeeping - the twilight signaled Maghrib prayer, the night determined sleeping and waking, the lunar phases defined their calendar. Using these as oaths connects divine truth to observable, undeniable natural phenomena.

The night “gathering” things references how at night, people return home, animals return to their dens, and the day’s activities come to an end. For the desert Arabs, nightfall meant gathering the family, securing the camels, seeking shelter. This would resonate deeply with the original audience’s daily experience.

Ayah 19

The phrase “stage upon stage” (tabaqan ’an tabaqin) reflects the historical reality of the Prophet’s ﷺ mission. The lecture identifies these stages explicitly: the persecution in Makkah, the Hijrah to Madinah, the battles of Badr, Uhud, Ahzab, Hudaybiyyah, and finally the conquest of Makkah. When this surah was revealed in early Makkah, the later stages were not yet visible - but the principle was being established that struggle proceeds through inevitable stages toward a conclusion.

Ayah 20-23

The refusal to prostrate when Quran is recited references the historical opposition of Quraysh leadership to the Prophet’s ﷺ message. They would physically walk away or make noise to prevent people from hearing the recitation (as described in Surah Fussilat 41:26).

The revelation that “Allah knows what they hoard” refers specifically to the economic system of Makkah. The Ka’bah served as a center of religious tourism, and each tribe’s idol brought pilgrims who conducted trade. This created a massive economic incentive to maintain the idolatrous system. Removing the idols threatened their entire economic model - the hospitality industry, the trade networks, the political security that came from tribes not attacking the sanctuary city. The Quraysh leadership wasn’t just defending theological beliefs; they were protecting a sophisticated economic and political system that benefited them enormously.

This pattern - religion being corrupted for political and economic gain - is not unique to pre-Islamic Arabia. The Biblical prophets repeatedly condemned the same corruption: the priests of Israel who perverted worship for gain (Jeremiah 6:13), the merchants in the temple whom Jesus ﷺ expelled (Matthew 21:12-13). The Quran’s critique of Quraysh fits into this prophetic tradition of exposing how religious authority can be exploited.

Ayah 25

The promise of reward “never discontinued” (ghayru mamnun) addresses a concern that would have been very real for the early Muslims: they were sacrificing their social standing, economic opportunities, and physical safety to follow the Prophet ﷺ. The promise that their reward will never end - never be cut off, never be held over them as a favor - provided crucial encouragement during persecution.

Coherence

Surah Structure

Surah Al-Inshiqaq is organized into three distinct sections:

Section 1 (Ayahs 1-5): The cosmic upheaval on the Day of Judgment - the sky cracking, the earth stretching and emptying itself. This section ends without the expected “then” clause, leaving the conclusion implicit.

Section 2 (Ayahs 6-15): The human journey and judgment - from struggling toward Allah, to meeting Him, to receiving the book of deeds, to final destinations. This section is precisely balanced between the righteous (Ayahs 7-9) and the wicked (Ayahs 10-15).

Section 3 (Ayahs 16-25): Divine oaths about natural cycles, followed by the emphatic statement that humans progress through stages, and the condemnation of those who deny this reality despite all evidence.

Connection to Previous Surahs

Al-Inshiqaq is explicitly identified in the lecture as the last in a series of surahs describing the Day of Resurrection, following At-Takwir (81) and Al-Infitar (82). Each surah in this series:

  • At-Takwir: Describes cosmic events (sun wrapped up, stars falling, seas overflowing) and concludes with “every soul will know what it brought forward”
  • Al-Infitar: Describes the sky being torn (futurat) and split, oceans erupting, graves being overturned, and concludes with “every soul will know what it sent forward and what it held back”
  • Al-Mutaffifin: Focuses on the books of deeds being recorded, with the righteous in ’Illiyun and the wicked in Sijjin
  • Al-Inshiqaq: Uses even stronger language (inshaqat - irreversible cracking) and uniquely OMITS the “then” clause, expecting the reader to already understand from the previous surahs

This progression shows the Quran building a concept across multiple surahs, each adding details while assuming knowledge of what came before. Al-Inshiqaq represents the culmination - the final, most emphatic warning, requiring no explanation of consequences because they’ve already been thoroughly established.

Angel Theme Across Surahs

The lecture identifies a sophisticated thematic thread about angels across these surahs: - Angels descending on Judgment Day and standing silent (An-Naba 78:38) - The angel that extracts souls (An-Nazi’at 79) - Angels that record and preserve revelation, particularly the Safarah (honorable scribes) in Al-Mutaffifin (83) - Angels that record human deeds - the Kiraman Katibin (honorable recorders)

By Al-Inshiqaq, angels are no longer mentioned. Instead, Allah says “inna rabbahu kana bihi basiran” - his Lord had been watching him all along. The angels were establishing the principle of accountability and witnessing, but ultimately accountability is directly to Allah. The angels are removed from the equation, leaving only the direct relationship between creator and created.

Internal Coherence: Nature and Human Response

The surah creates a powerful contrast:

Natural world’s response to Allah: - The sky is desperate to tear open, waiting for permission (adhinat - eager listening) - The earth wants to be stretched and throw out its contents, like a mother desperate to give birth (takhallat - relief) - Both respond immediately when Allah gives permission (wa huqqat - as they rightfully should)

Human response to Allah: - When Quran is recited to them, they don’t prostrate (la yasjuduna) - They don’t believe despite all the evidence (la yu’minuna) - They continuously call it all lies (yukadhhibuna)

The coherence is in the irony: unconscious creation desperately wants to respond to Allah’s command, while conscious humans who were given intellect and choice refuse to respond. The surah rhetorically questions: “What is wrong with them?” (fa ma lahum)

Connection to the Human Journey

The three natural phenomena in the oaths (twilight, night, moon) are not random - they each represent stages and processes: - Twilight (shafaq): The brief transitional period before darkness - there’s no stopping it - Night gathering (wasaqa): Things being enveloped and covered, the completion of a cycle
- Moon becoming full (ittasaqa): Gradual progression through phases to fullness

These directly parallel “latarkabunna tabaqan ’an tabaqin” - you will absolutely progress stage by stage. The natural world provides the evidence: just as day inevitably becomes night, just as the new moon inevitably becomes full, humans inevitably progress through life’s stages toward their meeting with Allah. The coherence is that the same divine pattern governs both natural phenomena and human existence.

Book Imagery Connection

The surah connects to Al-Mutaffifin’s extensive discussion of the recorded books (Sijjin and ’Illiyun). In Al-Mutaffifin, the focus was on where the books are kept and what’s written in them. In Al-Inshiqaq, the focus shifts to HOW the books are delivered - in the right hand versus behind the back. The book imagery that began developing in earlier surahs reaches its dramatic conclusion here with the actual handing over of the records.

Process and Finality

The entire surah is structured around the tension between gradual process and sudden finality: - The opening describes sudden, irreversible destruction (inshaqat, muddat, alqat) - The middle section describes the gradual journey (kadihun - struggling) - The oath section describes gradual processes (twilight fading, night gathering, moon waxing) - But all processes lead to inevitable conclusions

This coherence reinforces the surah’s central message: you are in a process right now (whether you acknowledge it or not), and that process has a definite end point. The question is not whether you’ll reach the end, but what state you’ll be in when you arrive there.

Psychological, Political & Social

The Psychology of Inevitability (Ayahs 1-6)

The opening ayahs create psychological discomfort by describing events that sound catastrophic but are actually presented as natural fulfillment of purpose. This challenges the human tendency to see the current state of things as permanent. When the surah says the sky has been “waiting” to crack open and the earth has been “pregnant” with the dead, wanting to be relieved of them, it reveals a psychological truth: we assume stability and permanence when in fact everything around us is in tension, held together only by divine decree.

The phrase “you are marching toward your Lord” (kadihun ila rabbika) addresses the human psychological need to feel in control. We make plans, we think we’re directing our lives, but the reality is we’re on a conveyor belt moving toward Allah whether we admit it or not. This can be psychologically liberating (for believers who trust Allah) or terrifying (for those trying to avoid this reality).

Social Dynamics of Greeting (Ayah 9)

The believer “returns to his family full of joy” (yanqalibu ila ahlihi masruran) addresses deep social and psychological needs. One of the greatest human fears about death is separation from loved ones. The promise of family reunion in Jannah speaks directly to this anxiety.

The ayah also reveals psychological wisdom about happiness: masrur (deeply felt happiness) is specifically the happiness of reunion and connection. True human joy is social and relational, not individualistic. The happiest moment isn’t receiving reward in isolation, but being able to share it with those you love.

The promise that Allah will remove all ill feelings (ma fi sudurihim min ghillin) addresses the psychological reality that even among believing families, there are tensions and hurts. Family gatherings can be sources of both joy and stress. The removal of all resentment transforms family relationships into their ideal form - pure love without the complications of hurt feelings, misunderstandings or conflicts.

Political Economy of Religion (Ayahs 20-23)

The surah’s statement “Allah knows what they hoard” (wa Allahu a’lamu ma yu’un) is a sharp political and economic critique. The lecture explains that the Quraysh’s resistance to Islam wasn’t merely theological - it was fundamentally about power and profit. The idols at the Ka’bah created:

Economic Benefits: - Religious tourism bringing all tribes to Makkah - Trade accompanying pilgrimage - Hospitality industry serving visitors - Economic monopoly on sacred trade routes

Political Benefits: - No tribe would attack Makkah because their idol was there - The Quraysh held power as custodians of the sanctuary - Tribal conflicts could be mediated through the religious authority of the Ka’bah

Removing the idols threatened to collapse this entire system. The Quraysh weren’t defending sincere religious belief; they were defending a sophisticated political and economic structure that enriched and empowered them. This reveals a timeless truth about how religious institutions can be corrupted for worldly gain.

The phrase “they call it all lies” (yukadhhibun) shows the psychological defense mechanism: when truth threatens your interests, the easiest response is total denial. They couldn’t afford to even consider the Prophet’s ﷺ message might be true, so they rejected it entirely without fair examination.

Social Pressure and Individual Accountability (Ayah 15)

“His Lord had always been watching him” (inna rabbahu kana bihi basiran) addresses a profound social-psychological dynamic. Humans are deeply social creatures who modify behavior based on who’s watching. We act differently when alone versus in company, when monitored versus unsupervised. This ayah reveals that Allah’s observation is constant and inescapable - there is never a moment when we’re truly “alone” or “unwatched.”

This has both positive and negative psychological implications: - Positive: For the righteous, this is comforting - Allah always sees your good deeds, even those done in secret, even those no one else appreciates - Negative: For the wrongdoer, this is terrifying - there’s no escape, no privacy for sin, no deed so secret that it’s truly hidden

The surah places this statement after describing someone who “thought he would never return” - revealing that people sin precisely because they believe they can escape accountability. The psychological reality is that humans need to believe they won’t face consequences for their actions in order to persist in wrongdoing.

The Psychology of Denial (Ayahs 13-14)

“He used to be joyful among his family, indeed he thought he would never return” connects happiness with false security. The person’s joy was based on delusion - he was happy because he convinced himself there are no consequences. This speaks to a psychological coping mechanism: people maintain happiness by avoiding thoughts of death and judgment.

The phrase “he was absolutely convinced” (innahu dhanna) uses dhann (assumption/conviction) rather than ’ilm (knowledge), revealing that his certainty was actually false confidence without real basis. Psychologically, the firmest-held beliefs are sometimes those with the least evidence - people cling most tightly to ideas that make them comfortable, regardless of their truth.

Social Consequences of Judgment (Ayah 10-12)

Receiving the book “behind his back” where he cannot see it reveals a social-psychological punishment: loss of self-knowledge. The person is denied even the dignity of knowing their own situation. Everyone around them can see their record, but they themselves are blind to it. This creates maximum humiliation - being exposed to others while being unable to defend yourself or even understand what’s being exposed.

The call for “thuburan” (destruction/death) rather than for mercy shows psychological insight: sometimes the pain of a situation becomes so unbearable that death seems preferable to continued existence in that state. Hell is designed to create this psychological state eternally - wanting death but being unable to die (“they will not die therein nor will they live” - 87:13).

Stages of Life and Loss of Control (Ayah 19)

“You will absolutely ride stage upon stage” addresses universal human anxiety about aging and loss of control. The lecture lists these stages: embryo, infant, child, student, adult, elderly, death, grave, resurrection. The psychological reality is that we have no control over this progression: - You cannot stay young - Your hair will gray (or fall out) regardless of what you do - Your eyesight will weaken - Your memory will fail - Your body will betray you

This creates existential anxiety, which people cope with in different ways: - Denial: Excessive focus on staying young, denying aging - Acceptance: Recognizing and submitting to the process - Faith: Trusting that the progression leads somewhere meaningful

The surah uses this inevitable progression to make its point: if you cannot stop the stages of life, how can you stop the stage of death and judgment that follows? The psychology is that accepting the reality of one inevitable thing should lead to accepting the reality of the next.

The Psychology of Easy Audit (Ayah 8)

The concept of “easy auditing” (hisaban yasiran) reveals profound psychological wisdom.
